coredata.dc:title: A simple method for preparing ZnO foam/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite and their photocatalytic applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Delei
Lan, Wei
Yang, Zhiwei
Zhao, Xiaohua
Chen, Youxin
Wang, Junya
Zhang, Xuetao
Zhang, Yue
Su, Qing
Xie, Erqing
coredata.dc:description: ZnO foam/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized by a facile process which ZnO foam prepared by combustion method was dispersed in CQDs solution. It is found that the ZnO foam is constructed by a large number of ZnO nanoparticles filling in irregular, hierarchical pores. The foam structure originates from a large amounts of gas produced in the combustion process. The obtained ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic activities on degrading Rhodamine B (RhB), Methyl orange (MO) and Methylene blue (MB) under UV and visible light irradiation. The order of degradation efficiency is MB>RhB>MO. It is attributed to the synergistic effect of several factors, including the light-trapping effect of ZnO foam, the up-converted photoluminescence behavior and photo-induced electron transfer property of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO foam
Combustion
Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Photocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite
Mechanisms of enhanced photocatalytic activity and process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The SEM image of ZnO foam. The inset is the photograph of as-prepared ZnO foam. (b) The SEM image of ZC2. The inset is the photograph of ZC2. (c) and (d) The high resolution SEM images of ZC2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) XRD patterns of ZnO, ZC1, ZC2 and ZC3. (b) The EDX analysis of ZC2.
The TEM images of ZC2 (a), CQDs (b) and ZnO crystal grain (c).
The UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnO, ZC1, ZC2 and ZC3.

The photocatalyst degradation of RhB with different ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites under UV (a) and visible light (c) irradiation. The degradation kinetic data of RhB under UV (b) and visible light (d) irradiation at different sampling time. The insets are the degradation photographs of RhB under UV and visible light irradiation, respectively.
The photocatalyst degradation of MB, RhB and MO with ZC2 and pure ZnO foam under visible light irradiation (a). The degradation kinetic data of MB, RhB and MO (b).

(a) Schematic diagram of the reflection and absorption of light in ZnO foam. (b) Up-converted spectra of CQDs with the excitation of visible and near infrared wavelengths. (c) Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic process of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: A simple method for preparing ZnO foam/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite and their photocatalytic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Magnetism Materials of Education, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Magnetism Materials of Education, Lanzhou University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnO foam/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized by a facile process which ZnO foam prepared by combustion method was dispersed in CQDs solution. It is found that the ZnO foam is constructed by a large number of ZnO nanoparticles filling in irregular, hierarchical pores. The foam structure originates from a large amounts of gas produced in the combustion process. The obtained ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic activities on degrading Rhodamine B (RhB), Methyl orange (MO) and Methylene blue (MB) under UV and visible light irradiation. The order of degradation efficiency is MB>RhB>MO. It is attributed to the synergistic effect of several factors, including the light-trapping effect of ZnO foam, the up-converted photoluminescence behavior and photo-induced electron transfer property of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para:
Nowadays, environmental problems and global energy crisis are the two major challenges in the human development, which have to be solved urgently. Semiconductor photocatalysts is a green technology to deal with the above-depicted two issues at the same time. Therefore, the research focused on semiconductor photocatalyst has attracted extensive attentions, because solar energy is inexpensive, environmental friendly, and inexhaustible [1–4]. A large number of oxide semiconductor photocatalysts have been studied widely, such as ZnO [5,6], SnO2 [7], TiO2 [8], CoO [9], BiVO 4
[10], Bi2WO6 [11]. However, photocatalytic efficiency is one of the biggest problems in application.

ZnO nanomaterials with low cost and non-toxicity have attracted extensive attention as a potential photocatalyst. But low photocatalytic efficiency and insufficient light harvest are two critical technique factors to be improved. Great efforts have been made to modify ZnO nanomaterials so as to enhance the photocatalytic performance, such as coupling with other semiconductor materials [12,13], doping impurities [14], creating different nanoscale morphologies [15,16] and so on [17–19].

Foam materials with porous structure are regarded as an attractive nanomaterial in the field of photocatalyst, because they can provide a large surface area and low light scattering due to the hollow architecture. The large surface area can increase the contact between photocatalyst and solution/gas. At the same time, the irradiated light can be reflected twice or more times in the hollow of foam materials, which produce more photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the photocatalytic efficiency would be efficiently improved. There are several methods for synthesizing foam materials, including template method, hydrothermal, sol–gel and combustion. Compared with the others, combustion technique is a facile route avoiding complex process and high cost, which is in favor of large-scale production. Therefore, it is worth exploring a combustion process to fabricate ZnO foam with excellent photocatalyst.

In terms of solar light utilization, ZnO can only absorb UV light due to its intrinsic wide band gap. It is found that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can act as a very useful component in the design of semiconductor photocatalysts due to its unique optical properties [20–23]. The up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CQDs can expand the light utilization range of nanomaterials from UV to visible range and enhance photo-induced electron transfer [24]. Yu et al. found the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 nanosheets was significantly enhanced due to the up-converted PL behavior [25]. Li et al. believed that the excellent photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under UV light irradiation originated from the promoted separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs based upon CQDs [26]. Di et al. also revealed that the excited high-energy electrons on CQDs modified BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets could quickly transfer to the CQDs and achieve efficient electron-hole pairs separation [27].

In this work, we first synthesized ZnO foam using one-step combustion method. The ZnO foam was dispersed in CQDs solution prepared by a simple hydrothermal synthesis. After dried at 80°C, ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite were obtained successfully. Rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO) and methylene blue (MB) were used as the target degradation to check the photocatalytic performance of ZnO foam and ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite. The formation mechanism of ZnO foam and the enhanced photocatalytic performance of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite were investigated in detail.

4.055g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved into 10ml ethylene glycol monomethyl ether with magnetic stirring at 65°C for 2h. The solution was transferred to a ceramic boat and dried into colloid state. After located in a furnace with 120°C for few minutes, the colloid started to burn tempestuously. ZnO foam could be successfully obtained. To further improve the crystalline, ZnO foam was annealed at 450°C for 2h in air.

0.75g sucrose was dissolved into 30ml deionized water. The syrup was transferred into a 40ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 185°C for 5h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The reactant solution was filtrated to separate the large carbon particles, and the brown filtrate was received as the CQDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: 100mg ZnO foam was dispersed in 10ml deionized water to obtain ZnO foam dispersion. 1g CQDs solution was dropped into 2ml deionized water to attain homogenous diluted CQDs solution. Then 0.1, 0.2 and 0.3g diluted CQDs solution were dropped into the ZnO foam dispersion with magnetic stirring for 2h, respectively. After that, the products were centrifuged and dried at 80°C for 12h. The obtained nanocomposites were labeled as ZC1, ZC2, ZC3.

The morphology, composition and microstructure of ZnO foam and ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX, Hitachi S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai-G2 F30). The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX-2400). The spectrum was measured at room temperature by an FLs920 steady state/transient state spectro xsort with xenon lamp. UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed by using a Hitachi U-3900H spectrophotometer.

The characterization method of photocatalytic performance had been reported in our previous works [28–30]. A certain amount of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite was dispersed into 50ml RhB solution (10mg/L). Before the irradiation, the dispersion was stirred in the dark about 1h to attain absorption-desorption equilibrium between RhB and the photocatalyst.

The degradation was carried out under xenon lamp (250W) with a UV filter and a cut-off filter (λ≥400nm), respectively. During the irradiation, 4.5ml dispersion was collected and centrifuged to remove the catalysts with a time interval. The filtrates were analyzed by recording the changes of the absorption bands (around 554nm) of RhB using a Hitachi U-3900H spectrophotometer. The control test is also carried out on commercial ZnO using the same procedure.

ZnO foam and ZC2 nanocomposite were also used to degrade MO and MB molecules under visible light irradiation with the similar procedures of RhB. Here the filtrates were analyzed by recording the changes of the absorption hands of MO (around 463nm) and MB (and 663nm) using a Hitachi U-3900H spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1a shows the SEM image of as-prepared ZnO foam using combustion process. The inset is the digital photograph of ZnO foam. It can be seen that the sample is ivory white and characteristic of a foam structure. Fig. 1b shows the SEM image of ZC2 nanocomposite. The color of ZC2 (the inset in Fig. 1b) turns into brown yellow compared with pure ZnO foam. But there is no obvious difference on the morphology between pure ZnO and ZC2. Fig. 1c and d are the high resolution SEM images of ZC2. The images show that the foam architecture is consisted of aggregated ZnO nanoparticles with the average diameter about 150nm. A lot of hierarchical pores with microscale and nanoscale can be found, which should be generated in the combustion process of precursor colloid. After reaching a critical temperature, the precursor colloid burned and caused the decomposition of Zn(NO3)2, which is induced by ethylene glycol monomethyl ignition [31]. At the same time, a large amount of gas, such as CO2, H2O, was produced in the combustible. The process of gas release formed a large number of hierarchical holes in the ZnO foam. During the annealing, the crystallization of ZnO foam was improved, and the cavity size was further increased with the volatilization of organic residues. The chemical reactions could be represented as follows:
20
C
3
H
8
O
2
+2Zn
( N
O
3
)
2
+75
O
2
→2ZnO+60C
O
2
↑+80
H
2
O↑+2
N
2
↑

Fig. 2a shows the XRD patterns of ZnO, ZC1, ZC2 and ZC3. It is found that the samples have the same characteristic diffraction peaks of wurtzite ZnO phase (PDF#36-1514). The peaks centered at 31.719°, 34.380°, 36.219°, 47.560°, 56.519°, 62.873°, 67.999° are corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112) crystal planes, respectively. It indicates that all the samples have a good crystallinity. There is no diffraction peaks related to carbon or other impurities in the XRD patterns. No signal related to carbon quantum dots is due to a small amount on the surface of ZnO foam. Fig. 2b shows the EDX spectrum of the ZC2. It can be seen that the sample is composed of Zn, O and C. The mass percent of C, O and Zn are 16.63%, 17.43% and 65.95%. It suggests that the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite ZC2 is mixed by CQDs and ZnO.

Fig. 3a shows the typical TEM image of ZC2, which reveals that the sample is consisted of ZnO nanoparticles and CQDs. The diameter of CQDs is in the range of several nanometers. It can be seen that CQDs are dispersed and attached on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles, and the density of CQDs is not so high. Fig. 3b and c show the HRTEM images of CQDs and ZnO crystal grain, respectively. The lattice spacing of 0.32nm is related with the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. The lattice spacing of 0.245nm corresponds well to the (101) plane of wurtzite ZnO. Therefore, the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites have been obtained successfully.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnO, ZC1, ZC2 and ZC3. The ZnO foam exhibits few absorption in the visible region. But the ZC1, ZC2 and ZC3 nanocomposites demonstrate a certain degree absorption in the visible region. The absorption intensity increases with increasing the amount of CQDs in the nanocomposite. The increase of the absorption intensity will be considered as one of the reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the visible range as the following section.

Fig. 5a shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB operated by the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite with different amount of CQDs under UV light irradiation. The self-decomposed of RhB is also shown for comparison. It can be seen that pure ZnO foam and all the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites exhibit prominent photocatalytic activity. The amount of CQDs has an obvious effect on photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic efficiency enhances with the increased amount of CQDs from ZC1 to ZC2. The optimal efficiency is obtained on the ZC2 nanocomposite. And then, the photocatalytic efficiency decreases from ZC2 to ZC3. Fig. 5b shows the degradation kinetic data that was fitted by a first order equation with a simplified Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [32]:
ln
(
C
0
C
t
)
= kt

where k is the reaction rate constant (min−1), C0 is the initial concentrations of RhB (10mg/L), Ct is the concentration of RhB after irradiating tmin. The k values of ZC1, ZC2, ZC3 and ZnO are 0.0450, 0.0827, 0.0670, 0.0304min−1, respectively. It can be further found that the ZC2 nanocomposite presents much more excellent UV photocatalytic capability. The digital photograph of the solution for degrading RhB with ZC2 under UV light irradiation at different sampling time (from 0 to 90min) is also shown in the inset of Fig. 5b.

Fig. 5c shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB with different ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. The self-decomposed of RhB and the control result of commercial ZnO are also shown in Fig. 5c. There is a little degradation of RhB in the presence of pure ZnO foam, which is attributed to the surface defect of the ZnO foam. After coupling with CQDs, the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites exhibit highly photocatalytic activity in the visible range. Moreover, the ZC2 nanocomposite also demonstrates the optimal photocatalytic efficiency in all the samples. Fig. 5d shows the degradation kinetic data. The k values of ZC1, ZC2, ZC3, ZnO foam and commercial ZnO are 0.0029, 0.0092, 0.0056, 0.0012 and 0.0006min−1, respectively. The degradation photograph of RhB with ZC2 photocatalyst under visible light irradiation at different sampling time (from 0 to 4h) is shown in the inset of Fig. 5c. The above-depicted results indicate that there is an optimal amount of CQDs in the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.

Fig. 6a shows the photocatalytic degradation of MB, MO and RhB with ZC2 and pure ZnO foam under visible light irradiation. Compared with RhB, there is also a little degradation for MB and MO in the presence of pure ZnO foam. The ZC2 nanocomposite shows the better photocatalytic activity in the visible light range for MB degradation. In case of MO, the degradation is slower than MB and RhB. Fig. 6b shows the degradation kinetic data. The k value of MB, RhB and MO with ZC2 are 0.0121, 0.0092 and 0.0031min−1, respectively. The degradation efficiency of the three dyes are in the order of MB>RhB>MO, which should be due to the intrinsic characteristics of dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of enhanced photocatalytic activity and process
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above results, pure ZnO foam and all the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites present prominent UV light photocatalytic activity, and ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites also present the outstanding visible light photocatalytic activity. Among them, ZC2 nanocomposite shows the optimal performance in UV–visible light range, which is due to the following two factors. The first is that ZnO foam structure is very conducive to optical absorption, as shown in Fig. 7a. The hierarchical porous structure with the microscale and nanoscale is capable of achieving multiple reflections of light to enhance the absorption efficiency. So the enhanced utilization rate of light could result in the improved photocatalytic efficiency. The second factor is the incorporation of CQDs into ZnO foam structure. The previous studies indicated that CQDs show an up-converted photoluminescence property [33,34]. That is CQDs can absorb visible light and near-infrared light, and then convert them to shorter wavelengths, which can expand the utilization scope of ZnO foam/CQDs photocatalyst from UV to visible region. Fig. 7b shows the up-converted property of CQDs measured by using excitation wavelengths ranging from 600 to 850nm. It can be found that the up-converted emission shows an excitation-dependent behavior. With the decrease of the excitation wavelength, the center of the emission light is shifted to the shorter wavelength. Using the excitation light of less than 650nm, the emission of UV light can be achieved. For example, the emission can be extended to 360nm for 600nm excitation light. Therefore, the up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs is very beneficial to improve the photocatalytic properties of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite.

Fig. 7c shows the schematic model for the photocatalytic process of ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. For the UV light irradiation, ZnO foam absorbs UV photons to excite photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In this case, the CQDs can be treated as electric sinks [35]. The photogenerated electrons transfer from the surface of ZnO foam to the CQDs. The recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be efficiently suppressed. Therefore, the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite presents the enhanced photocatalytic capability compared with pure ZnO foam. For the visible light irradiation, CQDs absorb visible photons and then converted them to UV photons by up-converted photoluminescence process, which can be absorbed by ZnO foam to improve the photocatalytic efficiency. The other processes are similar to the above-depicted UV light section. Therefore, the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites demonstrate the excellent UV and visible photocatalytic capability compared with pure ZnO foam.

Noted that there is an optimal load amount of CQDs in the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites. At first, the incorporated CQDs tend to uniformly disperse on the surface and pores of ZnO foam, which can enhance utilization rate of UV–visible light. However, the CQDs have no photocatalytic capability though they can absorb visible light. The higher amount of CQDs on the ZnO foam will result in a competition on the light absorption between ZnO foam and CQDs, which could lead to the decrease of photocatalytic capability for ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite. So it is very important to reasonably control the load amount of CQDs on the ZnO foam.

In summary, we have successfully prepared ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite by dispersing the combusted ZnO foam into CQDs solution. The ZnO foam is accumulated by a large number of ZnO nanoparticles filling in irregular, hierarchical pores. The ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite presents prominent UV and visible light photocatalytic activity. It is attributed to the light-trapping effect of ZnO foam, the up-converted property and electric sinks of CQDs. It is expected that the ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposite can provide a new insight into the design and promote practical applications of photocatalyst.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (No. 1208RJZA199), the fund of the State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Non-ferrous Metals, Lan Zhou University of Technology (SKLAB02014003), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. lzujbky-2015-116) and the Project sponsored by SRF for ROCS, SEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.mssp.2016.02.004.

ce:section.ce:para:
